speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 22, 1995. Survivor is Lily Feldman, F-E-L-D-M-A-N. Interviewer is Bonnie Slavin, S-L-A-V-I-N. The city is Miami Beach in the Miami, Florida district, USA. The language is English. This will be roll number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Bonnie Slavin, S-L-A-V-I-N. Today is August the 22nd, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Mrs. Lily Feldman of Miami Beach, Florida, the United States of America. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Lily Feldman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-L-Y F-E-L-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lily, again, Jacoby, J-A-C-O-B-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in London, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date, October the 4th, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you're the only person who knows it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Feldman-- Please--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, call me Lily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lily--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe what your family was like before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very traditional. In fact, less than traditional. I knew I was Jewish because my mother told me I was Jewish. We didn't belong to any particular synagogue. And when I was about four years old, four or five years old, we moved away from London, the London area, into the suburbs, in a section called Middlesex, which is the outskirts of London. And it was very non-Jewish. I think we were the only Jewish people in, in the whole area. We had a very nice house. And I had my own room, and it was lovely, and backyard. And I made friends with all the neighborhood children. And had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't, not at all. Wasn't Jewish at all. And there were no synagogues in the area. And it wasn't until the war broke out, actually, that I came in contact with any Jewish people and Jewish children. Because, because of the area being outside of the London, the real London area, people moved out to get away from the bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know you were Jewish as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother told me. And we used to have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Passover at my grandmother's house. But that's all we had. And we'd have a box of matzos in the house that week. But there was no, there was no changing of dishes or going through the rituals that I do now, of course. But my mother told me I was Jewish, and I knew I was Jewish. And my mother always instilled into me, from the time I could remember, never go out with a non-- when you get older, never go out with a non-Jewish boy. And God forbid, don't ever, ever marry a non-Jew. And this sort of stuck with me all my life. Well, and I knew-- and when I reflect on those times, I know I was different. I felt different, because everybody else knew I was Jewish. We didn't hide the fact. We just didn't do anything in the Jewish way. But we just told people we were Jewish, and I knew at school that I was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm the only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do as an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a hairdresser. And he was a wonderful, lovely, kind-- they called him the Pied Piper, because he had such a way with children. And whenever people, especially my friends, came to the house, they gravitated to my father, because he always had jokes and tricks. And he was just a lovely, lovely man. I think they came more for my father than for me, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother didn't work until the war. My mother was a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to in Ruislip, in Middlesex where we lived, I went to what they called Lady Bankes Junior Mixed, which was an elementary school. Lady Bankes was apparently a very famous lady in the history. And they named a school after her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you go to that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there from the age of six till I believe ten. And then the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, what was it like living in your neighborhood in your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice, very nice, yes. Very-- I didn't feel any antagonism, or-- I really--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience anti-Semitism at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I did, I did. At one time at Lady Bankes, my mother insisted that when they get-- had religious lessons that if it was concerning the New Testament, that I would be allowed to sit outside, which I did. However, one time somehow or other I didn't leave the room. And this particular teacher was discussing-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, probably it was around Easter time, and he was telling the story of Jesus and what happened to him. And then he made the comment that the Jews killed him. And then he pointed-- he said, the Jews, you know, just like Lily. And then the whole class sort of turned around and looked at me, as if I personally had killed Jesus Christ. And I was very, very distressed, and exceptionally distressed about that. And I came home, and I told my father. My father was a very, very placid man, very. Nothing upset him. He was such a wonderful, nice, kind person. And I have never seen him be so angry. He was very, very angry. And he went up the next day to the school and spoke to the headmistress, the principal. And he really was going to make a big stink about it. And he wanted to speak to his member of parliament and take it all the way to wherever you take it. And he was very, very upset about it. And the consequence was that I was removed from this particular teacher's class. And they had an A and B class. And at that time, I was in the B class. I got moved to the A class. And I thought, well it was just as well. Because I was-- I felt very good about going from the B to the A class.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the-- oh, and there was another time. I-- I didn't realize then that it was anti-Semitism. But I remember I had to go into hospital-- I can't remember exactly how old I was, probably six or seven, something like that. And I had to go into hospital, because in those days when you had-- I had scarlet fever-- and they quarantined you by putting you into the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went into hospital. And like every-- all hospitals, you have to say your religion. So naturally I was put down as Jewish. And the nurses called me Rebekah. And I couldn't understand why they called me Rebekah. And then I realized afterwards it was because I was Jewish. But I mean, they didn't do anything bad to me or-- but that was-- I realized years later that was a form of anti-Semitism. Subtle.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up before the war, what did you know about the rise of Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely nothing. I didn't know anything about it, no. It wasn't discussed. Oh, I mean if it was discussed, it wasn't discussed in front of me. When I was growing up, nothing was discussed in-- in front of children, they would be whispering about whatever. I mean, I remember an aunt of mine was pregnant. And I didn't even know she was pregnant until she had the baby. Because she sort of turned sideways, and I wasn't allowed to see her protruding stomach. I mean it was just very strange, but that's the way it was then.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you know about the war in Germany--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and what was taking place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew in 1939, September the 3rd, which was a Sunday, that war had broken out. And there was an air raid warning, a few minutes after the announcement, and everybody ran out. They said nobody go outside, because they were expecting bombs and fighting and dog fights and all that. Nothing happened. I mean it was a Sunday afternoon, and it was a very, very nice September day. And we heard this air raid warning. And we ran out to see what was going on, if anything. And absolutely nothing was going on, so of course everybody went back again. And my first recollection of actually the war was when they asked for volunteers to fill sandbags. And they were making announcements on the radio. And if you went to the cinema, you saw the newsreels. But I didn't know that there was anything particular-- at that time, I didn't know there was anything particularly bad going on with the Jews. My father tried to get-- once the Germans had marched into Holland, my father was from Holland. Once he had heard that the Germans had marched in, he tried to get in touch with his sisters. There was no question of making a telephone call. We didn't have a telephone. And the idea of making a phone call in those days just didn't occur.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wrote letters. And I remember we wrote frantic letters, and we never got any reply. And it was quite devastating, until eventually we did get our letters returned. And they were all stamped with swastikas and marked gone away, unknown, address unknown.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was very, very upset. But I didn't realize even then where they had gone or why they had gone. We just assumed that with the bombing and the shelling that they had been bombed out of their homes or whatever. And it wasn't until around-- actually till around 1942 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I heard a program on the radio about the concentration camps, that people had been there and they were sending messages. And I had heard it on the radio, this-- somebody, an announcer or some sort of program. And they were rounding up-- they said they were first of all rounding up the men and shooting the men. And my first feeling at that time was thank god they're not here, because they would have rounded up my father and shot my father. And if anything happened to my father, then, excuse me, life wouldn't be worth living. I just adored my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still adore my father. [SOBBING] So ridiculous, I can't think about him without getting all emotional. But he had that effect on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many relatives did he have in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had two sisters with their families and loads of cousins. His parents had passed away. And one of my aunts-- after the war, one of my aunts survived. And she made a comment which, at the time I thought was rather strange, but I can understand. She made a comment, thank god Mama and  Papa passed away before the war. Because I couldn't have stood it, if they would have had to go through what we went through. So that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the war changed your life after 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- It changed my life tremendously. Because my father never became a British subject. He kept his Dutch citizenship, although he was allowed-- he didn't need any work permits or anything like that, he was just a citizen. You know, like he just lived there. Resident, he was a resident.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had a house, so he paid taxes. And as a homeowner, he was allowed to vote. And he had all the comforts of being a British subject. And he said, what do I have to bother for?

speaker: interviewee
text: However, when the Germans marched into Holland and-- or the rest of Europe and Holland fell, and most of-- and all of Europe fell-- the British government in their wisdom, I suppose, decided that they wanted to keep tabs on all aliens, friendly or otherwise. The Germans who lived in England and the Italians were rounded up and put into-- not concentration camps, but into sort of separate camps, somewhere or other. Because I had a girlfriend in school whose father was from Italy, and he, like my father, never bothered to change his nationality. Her mother was English, and they took her father away. My father they didn't take away, they just gave him and my mother, and, of course, me too, three days to move out of our house. Because where we lived in the suburbs of-- of London, we were near an airport. Not really an airport, they called it an aerodrome, the North Hall Aerodrome, where the RAF were, were stationed. And it was not too far away from where we lived. And they were afraid that my father might, in the middle of the night, get up and go walk over there and blow it up or whatever. I don't know. But we were too close. Our house was too close to the, to the aerodrome. And they gave us three days to move closer to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was I think in 1940, we had to move. And, of course, we did. We gave up our house, and-- and they gave us certain areas where we could live, in London proper. And we moved there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what-- in the height of the Blitz. And we were in the middle of the Blitz. I mean, everybody was moving out of London, to get away from the Blitz, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? What did you feel at that time when you were moving? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, personally--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought it was great. Because I didn't realize how terrible it was. I-- I wasn't old enough, or maybe I wasn't smart enough. Maybe I was just stupid, I don't know. I was a little girl. And anything new and different was very interesting. And I like interesting new things, and I'm game for everything, more or less. I mean, I wouldn't go hang gliding or anything like that, but something different, I-- I didn't mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to move was fine. Good, we'll move. Go to a new school, go to-- didn't bother me at all, really didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we moved. We didn't have a house. We moved into what they call a flat, which was like a, a floor in a house. And we didn't spend too much time there. Because most of the time was spent in the air raid shelter, in the back garden. This house-- we lived upstairs, and another family lived downstairs. And when the government came to build these air raid shelters, anybody who had a-- a garden in England could apply for what they called an Anderson Shelter. And the government built it for you, or they, they put the-- they excavated it, and they gave you the stuff and you sort of built it yourself. And it was-- it was very, very safe to go down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Looked like an igloo, actually. Looked like an igloo. Built in-- the-- the top of the-- the shelter was rounded like an igloo. And there was an opening, and you went down. I think it was six feet down, and you went down. And you stayed there. And people decorated. We decorated it, and it was nice, it was comfortable. However, when we first moved to this flat, we had to share this Anderson Shelter, which-- they had different sizes. But apparently when they built it in this particular house, they built it just for this family, who were a mother, father, and two children. And they had an old mother living there who refused absolutely to go into the shelter. She wanted to stay into the-- in the-- in the flat, her flat. And she wouldn't go in. And then we came, and things got so bad. Of course bombs were flying, and the-- you could hear the planes overhead and just hope that they would go over and they wouldn't drop bombs. And then you'd hear the guns trying to shoot them out, and you'd see the-- the lights. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was, it was pretty scary. But I really wasn't-- I wasn't petrified, I really wasn't. I was just a little nervous. And we went to the shelter. And then I felt safe there. However, it was very uncomfortable. Because there were the other-- other family, and then we were there, too. So it was very, very uncomfortable. Until this family decided they weren't going to stay in London anymore. And they moved out. So they moved away, so then we had the shelter to ourselves. And then we made it very comfortable, put a sort of a-- a bed, a mattress there and-- and a-- a little bed for me and shelves. They told you not to put shelves up because the vibration from bombing made everything fall down. But we put shelves, because you had to you know, put your stuff away somewhere. And my mother used to take sandwiches and a thermos every, every night. And we would go down there, until one night there was a direct hit on the house where we lived. And we were bombed. And I was thrilled to pieces. I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't think why I was thrilled to pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 11. And I couldn't wait to get school to tell them. We've been bombed, we have nothing! Because all you saw were newsreels of all these people being bombed and crying and carrying on and-- and going to the underground, which was the subway. And it was just devastating to see them. But it was just them, and now I felt like I was one of them. I was-- I really felt that I was part of the war, and that they had included me in it. And anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family lose everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything, yes. We lost everything. It was completely devastated. The only thing, funnily enough, that was saved was our little refrigerator. We had-- we were one of the few families that, in those days, who had a refrigerator. It was a gas refrigerator. But the kitchen that we had in this flat wasn't big enough to hold the refrigerator, so they had fixed it up in the hallway, just outside the kitchen. And it was on a gas pipe. And when the house was hit, it just crumbled completely. And the roof was like on top of the rubble. And sort of hanging by this, this pipe, was our refrigerator, swinging in the breeze. So that was the only thing that was saved. Everything else was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that sound like when the bombs came?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, I was asleep. And my, my father woke me up. Because he heard it, apparently, coming down. And he sort of went to lay on top of me, in case the thing caved in, the shelter. But it didn't. The shelter was marvelous, that saved our lives. And next door was a family, was a lady with three children. Her husband was a baker, so he worked at nights in this bakery. And she was at home with the three children-- two little girls and a baby boy. And the only thing that was standing of the two houses was the back walls. And my mother heard the children crying. And she jumped up and ran next door, in the dark, to try and find these children. And she found them. And the mother was lying in bed, and apparently she couldn't move, because part of the ceiling had fallen down on her. And she was saying, I cannot move. I'm all right, but I just cannot move. I have a lot of rubble on me. And please take care of my children. And then the air raid wardens came and neighbors came from the other street. They came over the gardens, and they rescued us, which was very nice. And then my mother said to the-- these men, well, shine some lights and be careful, because there's a lady here who's lying, and she cannot move.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they-- they-- they released her. And we went, again, over, back-- gates and gardens and somebody in the next street took us all in. And naturally made a cup of tea, because in England, I mean, everything is with a cup of tea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was just great. I-- I was-- I thought it was just wonderful. It was a new experience for me. And then the next day, my mother wouldn't let me go to school, because I didn't have any clothes. So she sent me to my grandmother. My grandmother lived where we used to live, not too far away from where we used to live. And my mother, by being married to my father, was also prohibited from living in that neighborhood. However, because she was British-born, she had special dispensation to go-- I'm not sure if it was once a week or once a month-- to go and visit her mother. But I, as a child, was allowed to come and go, whenever I wanted. And they had a special identification card, saying that they were aliens. Which when you think about, it is rather ludicrous, especially for my mother. My mother was a, an English lady. So, anyway. Then after we were bombed and we had sold our house, they had checked my father and found out that he wasn't a spy, had never been a Nazi, wasn't in cahoots with the Nazis, was Jewish, and obviously wouldn't help the Nazis anyway. Then they told us, well, you can move back now. But we didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay with your grandmother for a period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed-- yes, I stayed with my grandmother for a week. And then I decided I wanted to be with my parents. And the British government-- I don't know if the government, the local counsel, my mother had gone to the town hall to say that she'd been bombed, and what are you going to do about it, and find me somewhere, and find me some, some clothes. Everything was rationed. And all clothing was-- I think they'd started with the clothing coupons in the-- I'm not sure, but anyway. And she was assigned this room with a kitchen in another street, near where, where we lived. And-- because housing was very, very difficult to come by in London. And you had to have permission to move and to go and to come and to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we-- they lived there for a week. And then I decided I wanted to go back and live with my parents, even if it was just, oh, one room.
